content: read reviews/404.md. if the summary section of the checklist review indicates the review passed, do nothing. if it failed, make relevant updates that are needed to deepmind/404.lean accordingly. if in doubt, reference CHECKLIST.md, which was the source document used when a previous instance of claude produced reviews/404.md.

    30→
    31→## 6. Theorem Statements
    32→
    33→- [x] Benchmark problems use the `theorem` keyword: both `erdos_404` and `erdos_404.variants.divergence`.
    34→- [x] Main theorem is named `erdos_404` (snake_case, matching the namespace number).
    35→- [x] Names follow Lean/Mathlib conventions (`snake_case` for terms of Props).
    36→- [x] Each theorem has both `@[category ...]` and `@[AMS ...]` attributes on the **same attribute line**: `@[category research open, AMS 11]`.
    37→- [x] Both theorems have docstrings.
    38→- [x] Docstrings reference the source paper: `[ErGr80, p.79]`.
    39→
    40→### `answer()` usage
    41→
    42→**`erdos_404` (Part 1):** The original problem asks "For which integers a ≥ 1 and primes p is there a finite upper bound..." The formalization conjectures that the bound exists for **all** a ≥ 1 and primes p, stated as a direct assertion. This is **Case 3** — no `answer()` wrapper needed.
    43→
    44→- [x] Theorem type is `P` directly (no `answer()` wrapper) — correct for a direct assertion.
    45→
    46→**`erdos_404.variants.divergence` (Part 2):** The original problem asks "Is there a prime p and an infinite sequence ... such that ...?" This is a yes/no question — **Case 1**.
    47→
    48→- [x] Theorem type is `answer(sorry) ↔ P` — correct for a yes/no question.
    49→- [x] The RHS formalizes the question as asked (existence of a prime and a strictly increasing sequence with diverging p-adic valuation of partial factorial sums), so `answer(True)` would mean "yes."
    50→- [x] Quantifiers (`∃ p, ...`) are on the RHS of `↔`, not as theorem parameters — compliant with the quantifier rule.
    51→
    52→## 7. Category Attribute
    53→
    54→- [x] Every theorem has exactly one `@[category ...]`.
    55→- [x] Values are valid: both use `research open`.
    56→- [x] Both `research open` theorems contain `sorry` in their proof bodies.
    57→
    58→## 8. AMS Attribute
    59→
    60→- [x] Every theorem has exactly one `@[AMS ...]` attribute.
    61→- [x] At least one AMS subject number is listed: `11`.
    62→- [x] Numbers are in ascending order (single number — trivially satisfied).
    63→- [x] No duplicate numbers.
    64→- [x] `11` is a valid AMS MSC2020 code (Number theory).
    65→
    66→## 9. Proof Bodies
    67→
    68→- [x] Open conjectures use `by sorry`: both `erdos_404` (line 60) and `erdos_404.variants.divergence` (line 78).
    69→- [x] No `sorry` in definitions (no definitions present).
    70→- [x] No informal proof sketches for open research problems.
    71→- [x] No `native_decide`.
    72→
    73→## 10. Variants & Multi-part Problems

    75→- [x] Variant is named `erdos_404.variants.divergence` — follows `erdos_N.variants.<descriptor>` convention.
    76→- [x] Variant lives in the same file as the main theorem.
    77→- [x] Variant has its own `@[category research open, AMS 11]` attributes.
    78→- [x] Variant has a docstring explaining its relationship to Part 1 ("expected to be a consequence of Part 1").
    79→
    80→## 11. Code Quality
    81→
    82→- [x] 2-space indentation throughout.
    83→- [x] Unicode math symbols used: `∀`, `∃`, `∈`, `≤`, `∧`, `∑`, `ℕ`, `→`, `↔`.
    84→- [x] No debug code (`#check`, `#eval`, `#print`, `dbg_trace`).
    85→- [x] No `set_option` that disables linters.
    86→- [x] Lines are reasonably short (all under 100 characters).
    87→- [x] File ends with a single trailing newline.
    88→
    89→## 12. Build Verification
    90→

    93→
    94→---
    95→
    96→**Verdict:** All checkable items pass. The file is clean and fully compliant with the checklist. Both the main theorem and the variant are well-formalized with appropriate `answer()` usage for their respective problem types. The docstrings are thorough, citing [ErGr80, p.79] and explaining the relationship between the two parts.
    97→
    98→**Note:** The original problem also asks "how does f(a,p) behave?" — this aspect is not formalized, but omitting it is reasonable given the difficulty of formalizing an open-ended question about function behavior.

file.content:
# Review: `deepmind/404.lean`

## 1. Copyright Header

- [x] File begins with the **exact** copyright block (no leading blank line).
- [x] Year is exactly `2026`.

## 2. Import Statement

- [x] Exactly one import line, immediately after the copyright block (separated by one blank line): `import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports` at line 17.
- [x] No other imports are present.

## 3. Module Docstring

- [x] A `/-! ... -/` module docstring appears after the import, separated by one blank line (line 19).
- [x] Starts with a heading: `# Erdős Problem 404`.
- [x] Contains a `*Reference:*` line with a markdown link to `erdosproblems.com/404`.
- [x] Includes a brief plain-English description of the problem (two sub-questions about factorial sums and p-adic valuations).
- [x] Bibliography entries present: `[ErGr80]` (Erdős–Graham) and `[Li76]` (Lin).

## 4. Namespace

- [x] All declarations are inside `namespace Erdos404 ... end Erdos404`.
- [x] Namespace name uses UpperCamelCase: `Erdos404`.
- [x] `open` / `open scoped` statements appear **before** the namespace block: `open Filter` (line 39) and `open scoped BigOperators` (line 41).

## 5. Custom Definitions

N/A — no helper `def`, `abbrev`, or `structure` declarations in this file.

## 6. Theorem Statements

- [x] Benchmark problems use the `theorem` keyword: both `erdos_404` and `erdos_404.variants.divergence`.
- [x] Main theorem is named `erdos_404` (snake_case, matching the namespace number).
- [x] Names follow Lean/Mathlib conventions (`snake_case` for terms of Props).
- [x] Each theorem has both `@[category ...]` and `@[AMS ...]` attributes on the **same attribute line**: `@[category research open, AMS 11]`.
- [x] Both theorems have docstrings.
- [x] Docstrings reference the source paper: `[ErGr80, p.79]`.

### `answer()` usage

**`erdos_404` (Part 1):** The original problem asks "For which integers a ≥ 1 and primes p is there a finite upper bound..." The formalization conjectures that the bound exists for **all** a ≥ 1 and primes p, stated as a direct assertion. This is **Case 3** — no `answer()` wrapper needed.

- [x] Theorem type is `P` directly (no `answer()` wrapper) — correct for a direct assertion.

**`erdos_404.variants.divergence` (Part 2):** The original problem asks "Is there a prime p and an infinite sequence ... such that ...?" This is a yes/no question — **Case 1**.

- [x] Theorem type is `answer(sorry) ↔ P` — correct for a yes/no question.
- [x] The RHS formalizes the question as asked (existence of a prime and a strictly increasing sequence with diverging p-adic valuation of partial factorial sums), so `answer(True)` would mean "yes."
- [x] Quantifiers (`∃ p, ...`) are on the RHS of `↔`, not as theorem parameters — compliant with the quantifier rule.

## 7. Category Attribute

- [x] Every theorem has exactly one `@[category ...]`.
- [x] Values are valid: both use `research open`.
- [x] Both `research open` theorems contain `sorry` in their proof bodies.

## 8. AMS Attribute

- [x] Every theorem has exactly one `@[AMS ...]` attribute.
- [x] At least one AMS subject number is listed: `11`.
- [x] Numbers are in ascending order (single number — trivially satisfied).
- [x] No duplicate numbers.
- [x] `11` is a valid AMS MSC2020 code (Number theory).

## 9. Proof Bodies

- [x] Open conjectures use `by sorry`: both `erdos_404` (line 60) and `erdos_404.variants.divergence` (line 78).
- [x] No `sorry` in definitions (no definitions present).
- [x] No informal proof sketches for open research problems.
- [x] No `native_decide`.

## 10. Variants & Multi-part Problems

- [x] Variant is named `erdos_404.variants.divergence` — follows `erdos_N.variants.<descriptor>` convention.
- [x] Variant lives in the same file as the main theorem.
- [x] Variant has its own `@[category research open, AMS 11]` attributes.
- [x] Variant has a docstring explaining its relationship to Part 1 ("expected to be a consequence of Part 1").

## 11. Code Quality

- [x] 2-space indentation throughout.
- [x] Unicode math symbols used: `∀`, `∃`, `∈`, `≤`, `∧`, `∑`, `ℕ`, `→`, `↔`.
- [x] No debug code (`#check`, `#eval`, `#print`, `dbg_trace`).
- [x] No `set_option` that disables linters.
- [x] Lines are reasonably short (all under 100 characters).
- [x] File ends with a single trailing newline.

## 12. Build Verification

- [ ] `lake build` — not verified in this review.
- [ ] All five custom linters pass — not verified in this review.

---

**Verdict:** All checkable items pass. The file is clean and fully compliant with the checklist. Both the main theorem and the variant are well-formalized with appropriate `answer()` usage for their respective problem types. The docstrings are thorough, citing [ErGr80, p.79] and explaining the relationship between the two parts.

**Note:** The original problem also asks "how does f(a,p) behave?" — this aspect is not formalized, but omitting it is reasonable given the difficulty of formalizing an open-ended question about function behavior.

content.text:
The review passed — all checkable items have checkmarks, and the verdict says "All checkable items pass. The file is clean and fully compliant with the checklist." The only unchecked items are build verification (items 12), which are noted as "not verified in this review" and are not failures.

No changes needed to `deepmind/404.lean`.
